title: Master These Habits And You'll Never Be Bothered By Anything
description:
In this episode, I break down why real power comes from staying calm when someone tries to trigger you, and why forcing calm often backfires.

If you want to respond with clarity instead of reacting emotionally, this is the playbook.
transcript:
Picture this. Someone tries to trigger
you. They raise their voice. They try to
get a reaction out of you. And instead
of getting angry or defensive, you stay
so calm, it makes them nervous. That's
real power. The power of not reacting. I
have seen it decide the outcomes of
negotiations, change power dynamics in
the boardroom, make or break careers.
Power doesn't belong to the loudest
person in the room. It belongs to the
one who never loses their calm. So, in
this video, I'm going to show you how to
never lose your calm under pressure and
master the frameworks that let you stay
unshaken inside and let nothing affect
you. But here's the first thing that
will surprise you. The harder you try to
stay calm, the more anxious you get.
Most people misunderstand calm. They
think calm is something you force, like
flipping a switch. You know, the
psychologist Daniel Wgner ran an
experiment. He told people, "Whatever
you do, don't think of a white bear."
What happened? Everyone immediately
thought of a white bear. The command to
suppress something becomes the trigger
itself. He called it ironic process
theory. Calm works the same way. The
moment you tell yourself don't panic,
your brain grabs the word panic and
starts panicking. And now suppression
becomes an amplifier. And why does
stress fire in the first place? Science
tells us that when your brain observes
that the demands of the situation exceed
the resources it has, it triggers
stress. It's not just what's happening,
it's whether you believe you can handle
what's happening. Here's what most
people miss. Calm isn't performed in the
moment. It's built before the moment.
And calm also gives you a hidden
advantage. Let's talk about that next.
Staying calm is your biggest power move.
Imagine you're the pilot of a commercial
flight. Early morning, half the cabin is
half asleep. Then bang, you know, birds
slam into both engines, both engines
fail, the plane starts dropping. What do
you do next? That was the exact
situation Captain Sully Sullenberger was
facing. He didn't have much time at all.
In that moment, air traffic control
didn't matter. The checklist didn't
matter. What mattered was his own
emotional temperature. He had to choose
to land the plane on the Hudson River.
And 90 seconds before the impact, he got
on the intercom and spoke in a very calm
voice that was direct and professional.
No shaking, no tremble, no panic hiding
between the words because he knew the
cabin would mirror his state. He stayed
very composed. He landed the plane on
the river and saved 155 lives. Sully
controlled himself first before
controlling the plane. That's the
sequence that matters because he knew a
vital truth. In a crisis, people don't
follow your words, they mirror your
state. A leader who panics spreads
panic. A leader who stays composed
stabilizes the room. Now, let's talk
about the framework that builds that
kind of calm before the storm hits. The
first framework is about creating a gap
where most people collapse it. I call it
the three spaces. I once asked my music
teacher how he could take dramatic risks
on stage, improvising insane rhythmic
combinations in real time on the fly.
and he said, "Well, when I'm on stage,
I'm not threatened." His answer was less
about music and more about his mindset.
He wasn't making choices to protect his
ego. He was just letting go. When we're
threatened, our stress level rises. Our
brain gets flooded with high level of
stress hormones. Our brain switches
focus from the CEO part of the brain,
which is the prefrontal cortex, to the
amygdala, your alarm system. and you
stop thinking like a CEO and start
reacting on instinct. So if you want to
stay calm under pressure, then your CEO
function has to still remain online,
right? How you create three spaces that
most people never find. Space one is
between stimulus and response. Victor
Frankle survived the concentration camp
by living in this very gap. the space
between what happened to him and how he
chose to respond. That space is very
tiny. Something happens, most people
react instantly, but that space is
everything. If you can separate what's
happening to you from what you do next,
you stay in control. Space two is
between urgency and importance. Stress
tends to compress time. When you're
under pressure and when the cortisol
level is high, you'll want to react
urgently. you'll be more prone to
interrupting others, rushing into
decisions, thinking in black and white,
and overreacting in general. So, you
have to create that space. And you do
that by slowing down the reaction time
and by separating what feels urgent from
what actually is important. And finally,
space three is between identity and
outcome. This is the hardest gap. When
your ego is fused to the outcome,
everything becomes personal. Any
disagreement feels like disrespect.
Minor flaws feel like major failures.
There is a study out of UCLA that found
that social rejection can activate the
same pain related circuits in the brain.
So your brain treats any rejection or an
attack on your status just like a knife
wound. If you separate who you are from
what's happening to you, you'll still
remain calm every time the stakes get
high. But we can only be calm when our
ego isn't on trial. Those are the three
spaces. So how do you make this
actionable right now? The best and
fastest biological circuit breaker is
the physiological sigh. Because when you
stress, your breathing gets shallow,
carbon dioxide builds up, and your heart
rate climbs. You can't think your way
out of it, but you can breathe your way
out. Double inhale through the nose and
one long exhale through the mouth.
That's physiological sigh. This is the
fastest reset to your nervous system. It
drops your physiological stress quickly
and that triggers a downshift for your
stress response. Knowing these gaps is
the foundation. But what do you do when
the pressure hits? You change the frame
before you change your feelings. You
know, Mike Tyson nailed it when he said,
"Everyone has a plan until they get
punched in the mouth. What matters is
what you do next." On my first job back
in Boston, I was told to turn a
boardroom into a demo center. And I had
worked on it for weeks. We were showing
it to the CEO at 10:00 a.m. And when I
arrived at 9:30, my boss Paul was bright
red and fuming. How could you show up
just 30 minutes before? Do you
understand? This is your first
interaction with the company's CEO? He
was raging mad. And I stayed quiet. I
apologized and I moved on. The CEO came.
The demos worked. Okay. Later, I
realized that I had the right context,
but I couldn't access it under pressure.
I didn't tell my boss that I had been in
that room until 4:30 a.m. in the morning
making sure everything was perfect,
everything was working. And that's the
problem. You always find the best
response later when you're calm. So, how
do you stay present and not feel
threatened? I use a four-step framework
I call calm. Control the frame, absorb
the hit, lower the stakes, move
decisively. Calm. C is control the
frame. This is the most important first
step because we all react to the same
moment in completely different ways,
right? Some escalate, some freeze, I go
quiet. But high performers ask before
reacting. What game is being played?
What is the actual stake? Is this threat
real or just loud? Most existential
threats you will find are temporary
misalignments. So control the frame by
naming what's actually at stake. Label
it. A absorb the hit. When pressure
hits, you may not prevent the first
spike, but you can control what happens
next. This doesn't mean you take abuse
silently, but let it land without
returning fire, then reorient. See the
pressure building as raw data, downshift
it internally. For instance, if someone
comes at me nowadays, I reduce it to
physics. I think, well, these are just
sound waves, not a verdict. And that
allows me to focus on the message and
the intention without absorbing the
heat. It keeps me present because you
have to give your brain those few
seconds to come back online. Third is L.
Lower the stakes. Remind yourself this
is a conversation or a discussion or an
argument. There's no bear charging at
you. This is not a personal threat. So
that lowers the stakes. Just because
they are triggered doesn't mean you have
to be. I do this at home sometimes. I
misread a remark, make it personal, and
withdraw. That's not calm. That's me
being passive aggressive. Remember to
ask for clarification before making
assumptions. And you know, this matters
even more nowadays because our digital
life trains us to misread tone and
intention constantly. Finally, M. Move
decisively. Act once clearly without
emotional baggage. One clear action, one
clean sentence. When you finally make
your move, it should set the tone. When
you react with compassion, with calm,
you can change the room. It's like
playing tennis. You served, the ball is
in their court. Now they have to respond
to your calm. That's leverage. So that's
the framework. Calm is how you stop
being hijacked. Most people react. High
performers reframe. Control the frame,
control the room. And finally, calm
isn't a personality trait. It's a
survival skill because we're living
inside two storms that destabilize us
constantly. What are the two storms that
surround us at once nowadays? First, the
social media storm. It's designed to
keep us in a constant state of rage and
anxiety because controversy pays better
than calm. And second, the AI storm. And
that one is injecting real fear, real
uncertainty and doubt in all of us about
the very future of work, about the very
nature of work. So yes, staying calm is
harder nowadays than it used to be. But
high performers learn to stay focused
anyway. In 2008, for example, while
markets were melting down, Warren
Buffett wrote in New York Times, seven
famous words, bad news is an investor's
best friend. He stayed disciplined and
looked for opportunities where others
saw catastrophe. And here's what makes
this super hard. I knew his playbook. I
understood the math. I saw the signal. I
read that article and I still sold at
the worst time at the bottom of the
market and I bought back at the peak and
it cost me real money. So the good news
is that we can train ourselves to
weather these storms. The system can be
built. The bad news, it takes time and
deep, deep self-awareness. And the
hardest part is the ego. Separating who
you are from what's happening to you.
The question isn't how do I stay calm?
It's what am I trying to protect here?
So, at a deeper level, calm is a
prerequisite for resilience. When you
feel like life is weighing down on you
or you're being swept away or thrashed
around by the currents of life, calm is
what lets you find your stable ground
and from that ground you can steady
others as well. There is a beautiful
Sanskrit line.
Who can conquer the world? The one who
conquers the mind because resilience
isn't enduring storms. is realizing the
only storm that matters is the one
raging inside of you. Calm that storm
and the rest gets quieter. If this
helped, please subscribe and check out
this video where I break down other
lessons I learned inside billion-dollar
boardrooms. See you next week. Thank you
and I love
